schools.summary: The offence of Pharaoh's butler and baker: 'and it came to pass after these things, that the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker had offended their lord the king of Egypt' — Pharaoh's chief butler (cupbearer) and chief baker (high officers of the royal household) offending (sinning against, displeasing) the king (the great officers fallen under the king's displeasure, the providence bringing them to the prison where Joseph was, vv.2-3, the occasion of the dreams); the offence — some fault (perhaps suspected treachery, or some failure in their service) that angered Pharaoh (the displeasure of the king, the officers cast into prison, v.3); the offence and imprisonment of the two officers, by God's providence, bringing them to Joseph's prison (vv.3-4), where their dreams, vv.5-19, and Joseph's interpretation would, in time, lead to his deliverance (41:9-13, the providence weaving the means of Joseph's exaltation) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass after these things, that the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker had offended their lord the king of Egypt
the offence of Pharaoh's butler and baker
the great officers fallen under the king's displeasure
the providence bringing them to Joseph's prison (41:9-13)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's wrath against his two officers: 'and Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers, against the chief of the butlers, and against the chief of the bakers' — Pharaoh angry with the chief butler (cupbearer) and chief baker (the heads of those departments of the royal household, the king's wrath at their offence, v.1, the great officers under the royal displeasure, the anger that led to their imprisonment, v.3); the two officers — the chief butler (who would be restored, v.21) and the chief baker (who would be hanged, v.22, the high officers, the heads of the royal service); Pharaoh's anger (the imprisonment of the two officers) by God's providence the means of bringing them to Joseph's prison (v.3, the providence ordering the king's wrath to weave Joseph's deliverance, 41:9-13, the divine hand in the affairs of kings) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Pharaoh was wroth against two of his officers, against the chief of the butlers, and against the chief of the bakers
Pharaoh's wrath against his two officers
the chief butler (to be restored, v.21) and chief baker (to be hanged, v.22)
the providence ordering the king's wrath (41:9-13)

schools.summary: The officers imprisoned where Joseph was: 'and he put them in ward in the house of the captain of the guard, into the prison, the place where Joseph was bound' — Pharaoh imprisoning the two officers in the house of the captain of the guard (Potiphar, 39:1) — the very prison where Joseph was held (39:20, the providence bringing the officers to Joseph, the meeting that would lead to the dreams, vv.5-19, and Joseph's deliverance, 41:9-13); the prison in the house of the captain of the guard (Potiphar, or his successor) — the state prison (39:20, the place of Joseph's imprisonment, the providence ordering the officers there); 'the place where Joseph was bound' (the officers brought to the very prison where Joseph was, the providence placing them under Joseph's care, v.4, the meeting that would, by the dreams, lead to Joseph's release, 41:9-13, the divine weaving of the means) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And he put them in ward in the house of the captain of the guard, into the prison, the place where Joseph was bound
the officers imprisoned where Joseph was
the providence bringing them to Joseph's prison (39:20)
the meeting that would lead to his deliverance (41:9-13)

schools.summary: Joseph charged to serve the officers: 'and the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he served them: and they continued a season in ward' — the captain of the guard (Potiphar, or the prison-keeper acting for him) charging Joseph to attend the two officers, Joseph serving (waiting on) them (the providence bringing the officers under Joseph's care, the meeting that would lead to the dreams, vv.5-19); Joseph set over the officers (his trust in the prison, 39:22) and serving them — the faithful, humble service (the godly young man attending the fallen officers, the providence placing him with them); 'they continued a season in ward' (the officers in prison some time, the season during which the dreams came, v.5, the providence preparing the occasion of Joseph's interpretation, and his deliverance, 41:9-13, the waiting) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he served them: and they continued a season in ward
Joseph charged to serve the officers
the godly servant attending the fallen officers (39:22)
the providence preparing the occasion of the dreams (v.5) and his deliverance

schools.summary: The butler and baker each dream a dream: 'and they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dream, the butler and the baker of the king of Egypt, which were bound in the prison' — the butler and baker each dreaming a dream the same night (the two dreams, vv.9-11, 16-17, the providence sending the dreams, as God spoke by dreams, Job 33:15; Numbers 12:6, the divine communication, each dream with its own meaning); each dream having its own 'interpretation' — a particular meaning (the butler's of restoration, the baker's of death, vv.12-19, the dreams not idle, but significant, the providence giving each its sense, the meaning that Joseph, by God, would declare); the two officers, in prison, troubled by their dreams — the providence sending the dreams to the very men under Joseph's care, that he might interpret them (vv.8-19) and, by the butler's later remembrance, be delivered (41:9-13, the divine weaving of the means of Joseph's exaltation) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And they dreamed a dream both of them, each man his dream in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dream...
the butler and baker each dreaming a dream
the dreams sent by God (Job 33:15), each with its own interpretation (vv.12-19)
the providence weaving the means of Joseph's deliverance (41:9-13)

schools.summary: Joseph finds the officers sad: 'and Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them, and, behold, they were sad' — Joseph, attending the officers (v.4), coming in the morning and seeing they were sad (troubled by their dreams, v.5, the observant, kind care of Joseph, the noticing of their distress, the occasion of his inquiry, v.7); Joseph 'looked upon them' (observed them) — the attentive, compassionate care of the godly servant (the noticing of their countenance, the kindness that led him to ask the cause, v.7, the considerate Joseph); 'behold, they were sad' (the officers troubled, their dreams, v.5, disturbing them, with no interpreter, v.8, the sadness Joseph observed, the occasion of his inquiry and the interpretation, vv.7-19, the providence) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph came in unto them in the morning, and looked upon them, and, behold, they were sad
Joseph finding the officers sad
the attentive, compassionate care of the godly servant
the occasion of his inquiry and the interpretation (vv.7-19)

schools.summary: Joseph asks the cause of their sadness: 'and he asked Pharaoh's officers that were with him in the ward of his lord's house, saying, Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?' — Joseph, seeing them sad (v.6), kindly asking the cause (the considerate inquiry of the godly servant, the compassion that drew out their trouble, the dreams, v.8, the occasion of the interpretation); the officers in the ward (prison) with Joseph (vv.3-4) — the fallen butler and baker (the men under Joseph's care, the troubled prisoners); 'Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?' (Joseph's kind, attentive question, the compassion for the troubled, the inquiry that led to the dreams, v.8, and the interpretation, vv.12-19, the considerate care that, by God's providence, opened the way to his deliverance, 41:9-13) — Gill.

themes: And he asked Pharaoh's officers that were with him in the ward of his lord's house, saying, Wherefore look ye so sadly to day?
Joseph asking the cause of their sadness
the kind inquiry of the godly servant
the compassion that drew out their dreams (v.8) and led to his deliverance (41:9-13)

schools.summary: Joseph ascribes interpretation to God: 'and they said unto him, We have dreamed a dream, and there is no interpreter of it. And Joseph said unto them, Do not interpretations belong to God? tell me them, I pray you' — the officers troubled by their dreams (v.5) and having no interpreter (in the prison, away from the Egyptian magicians and diviners they would have consulted, the distress of the unread dreams, the occasion of Joseph's word); Joseph's great word — 'Do not interpretations belong to God?': Joseph ascribing the power of interpreting dreams to God alone (not to the Egyptian magicians, nor to himself, God alone, the giver of the dreams, and of all revelation, can declare their meaning, the humility of Joseph, not boasting his own skill, but pointing to God, the revealer of secrets, Daniel 2:28, the godly disclaimer — interpretations are God's, and what Joseph would speak would be by God's gift, as he confessed before Pharaoh, 41:16, 'It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace,' the ascribing of all to God); 'tell me them, I pray you' (Joseph offering to hear the dreams, confident that God would give the interpretation through him, the faith that God would reveal, the humble, God-honoring offer, the interpretation to follow, vv.12-19, by God's gift) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'Do not interpretations belong to God?' display?

sides.position: Joseph ascribing the power to interpret dreams (and all revelation) to God alone, not to the magicians nor to himself - the humility that pointed to God the revealer of secrets (Daniel 2:28; 41:16), and the faith that God would reveal through him

themes: And they said unto him, We have dreamed a dream, and there is no interpreter of it. And Joseph said unto them, Do not interpretations belong to God? tell me them, I pray you
Joseph ascribing interpretation to God (the crux of God the revealer)
interpretations belong to God alone, not the magicians nor himself (Daniel 2:28; 41:16)
the humility that pointed to God, and the faith that God would reveal through him

schools.summary: The chief butler tells his dream of the vine: 'and the chief butler told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, In my dream, behold, a vine was before me' — the butler recounting his dream: a vine before him (with three branches, budding, blossoming, bearing ripe grapes, vv.10-11, the dream of the cupbearer, fitting his office, the vine and the cup, the occasion of Joseph's interpretation, vv.12-13); the vine — the central image of the butler's dream (the vine, the grapes, the cup, fitting his service, the dream Joseph would interpret, vv.12-13, the symbol of his restoration); the butler's dream (the vine, vv.9-11), sent by God (v.5), would be interpreted by Joseph (vv.12-13) as restoration (the providence using the dream to lead to Joseph's deliverance, 41:9-13, the means woven) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And the chief butler told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, In my dream, behold, a vine was before me
the chief butler telling his dream of the vine
the dream of the cupbearer (the vine and the cup), sent by God (v.5)
which Joseph would interpret as restoration (vv.12-13)

schools.summary: The three branches of the butler's vine: 'and in the vine were three branches: and it was as though it budded, and her blossoms shot forth; and the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes' — the butler's dream: a vine with three branches, which budded, blossomed, and bore ripe grapes (the rapid growth in the dream, the three branches, the three days, v.12, the vine bearing fruit, fitting the cupbearer's restoration, the dream Joseph would interpret); the three branches — which Joseph would interpret as three days (v.12, the symbol of the time to the butler's restoration, the detail of the dream); 'the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes' (the vine bearing ripe grapes, the butler pressing them into Pharaoh's cup, v.11, the dream of the cupbearer's service restored, the symbol of his restoration to office, v.13, the providence in the dream) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And in the vine were three branches: and it was as though it budded, and her blossoms shot forth; and the clusters thereof brought forth ripe grapes
the three branches of the butler's vine
the three branches (the three days, v.12), the vine bearing ripe grapes
the symbol of the cupbearer's restoration (v.13)

schools.summary: The butler pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup: 'and Pharaoh's cup was in my hand: and I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand' — the butler dreaming of doing his cupbearer's office: pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup and giving it to the king (the dream of his service restored, the very act of his office, the symbol of his restoration, v.13, the dream Joseph would interpret); the act of the cupbearer — pressing the grapes, giving the cup (the ancient manner, or the dream's image of his office, the service of the butler restored, the symbol of his return to favor); 'I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand' (the butler serving Pharaoh again, in the dream, the symbol of his restoration to his office, v.13, 'Pharaoh shall... restore thee unto thy place, and thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand,' the dream fulfilled, v.21, the providence) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Pharaoh's cup was in my hand: and I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand
the butler pressing the grapes into Pharaoh's cup
the cupbearer's office restored in the dream
the symbol of his return to favor (vv.13, 21)

schools.summary: Joseph interprets the three branches as three days: 'and Joseph said unto him, This is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three days' — Joseph (by God, v.8) interpreting the butler's dream: the three branches (v.10) are three days (the interpretation given confidently, as from God, the meaning declared, the symbol read); Joseph giving the interpretation with assurance (as from God, v.8) — not a guess, but the certain meaning revealed (the confidence of the God-taught interpreter, the meaning declared); 'The three branches are three days' (the three branches, v.10, signify three days, to the butler's restoration, v.13, the symbol interpreted, the time to the fulfillment, the providence revealing the meaning through Joseph) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph said unto him, This is the interpretation of it: The three branches are three days
Joseph interpreting the three branches as three days
the God-given meaning, declared with certainty (v.8)
the time to the butler's restoration (v.13)

schools.summary: Joseph foretells the butler's restoration: 'yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place: and thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand, after the former manner when thou wast his butler' — Joseph foretelling that in three days Pharaoh would 'lift up the butler's head' (restore him, raise him to honor — the phrase used favorably here, but ominously of the baker, v.19) and restore him to his office (the interpretation of the dream, the three branches, v.12, the restoration foretold); the phrase 'lift up thine head' — to raise to honor, restore to office (favorable here, the contrast with the baker, v.19, 'lift up thy head from off thee,' hanging, the same phrase, opposite issue, the restoration of the butler); 'thou shalt deliver Pharaoh's cup into his hand, after the former manner when thou wast his butler' (the butler restored to his cupbearer's office, as in his dream, v.11, the exact fulfillment, v.21, the interpretation that proved true, the providence, and Joseph's God-given gift, declaring the future) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thine head, and restore thee unto thy place... after the former manner when thou wast his butler
Joseph foretelling the butler's restoration
'lift up thine head' (to honor here, but the baker's hanging, v.19)
the cupbearer's office restored, fulfilled (v.21)

schools.summary: Joseph asks the butler to remember him: 'but think on me when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindness, I pray thee, unto me, and make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house' — Joseph, having interpreted the dream of restoration (v.13), asking the butler to remember him when restored, and to show him kindness (the lawful use of means, Joseph seeking, through the butler, his release, the request to be remembered, the godly man using means while trusting God); Joseph asking the butler to 'make mention of me unto Pharaoh' — to speak of his case to the king (the lawful seeking of deliverance through the butler's influence, the use of the means God afforded, yet the butler would forget, v.23, the appeal for help); 'bring me out of this house' (Joseph seeking release from the prison, the desire for deliverance, the innocent longing to be free, the lawful means, not wrong to seek it, while waiting on God, the request that, though forgotten, v.23, would in God's time be answered, 41:9-13) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: But think on me when it shall be well with thee, and shew kindness, I pray thee, unto me, and make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house
Joseph asking the butler to remember him
the lawful use of means, seeking release through the butler (yet forgotten, v.23)
the godly man using means while waiting on God (answered in his time, 41:9-13)

schools.summary: Joseph's plea of his innocence: 'for indeed I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews: and here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon' — Joseph declaring he had been stolen (kidnapped, by his brothers' sale, 37:28) from the land of the Hebrews (Canaan, where his fathers dwelt, the wrong done him, sold into slavery, the innocent man's account of his coming to Egypt, the plea of his unjust suffering); 'the land of the Hebrews' (Canaan, where Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob dwelt as Hebrews, Joseph's homeland, the place from which he was stolen, the kindred of the patriarchs); 'here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon' (Joseph protesting his innocence, he had done no wrong to deserve the prison, the false accusation, ch. 39, the plea of the unjustly imprisoned, the innocent suffering wrongfully, 1 Peter 2:19, the appeal to his blamelessness, both in being sold and in being imprisoned) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB.

themes: For indeed I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews: and here also have I done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon
Joseph's plea of his innocence
stolen from his home (the sale, 37:28), the wrong done him
the innocent suffering wrongfully (ch. 39; 1 Peter 2:19)

schools.summary: The chief baker tells his dream of the baskets: 'when the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he said unto Joseph, I also was in my dream, and, behold, I had three white baskets on my head' — the baker, hearing Joseph's favorable interpretation of the butler's dream (vv.12-13), encouraged to tell his own (hoping for a like good interpretation, the favorable reading emboldening him, the occasion of his dream's telling); the baker's dream — three white (or wicker) baskets on his head, the top one with baked goods for Pharaoh, the birds eating from it (v.17, the dream of the baker, fitting his office, the baskets and bread, the three baskets, the three days, v.18, the dream Joseph would interpret, ominously, vv.18-19); 'three white baskets on my head' (the three baskets, the three days, v.18, the baked meats for Pharaoh, the baker's office, yet the birds eating, the ominous sign, v.17, 19, the dream whose interpretation would be death, vv.18-19, not restoration) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he said unto Joseph, I also was in my dream, and, behold, I had three white baskets on my head
the chief baker telling his dream of the baskets
the baker encouraged by the butler's good interpretation (vv.12-13)
the three baskets (the three days, v.18), yet his dream would mean death (vv.18-19)

schools.summary: The birds eating from the baker's basket: 'and in the uppermost basket there was of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh; and the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head' — the baker dreaming of baked meats (for Pharaoh) in the top basket, and birds eating them out of the basket on his head (the ominous detail, the birds devouring the king's food, a sign of the baker's coming death, v.19, the birds eating his flesh, the dream Joseph would interpret as death); the baked goods for Pharaoh (the baker's office, as the butler's cup, v.11, the food prepared for the king, the symbol of his service, yet undone by the birds); 'the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head' (the ominous sign, the birds devouring the food, presaging the birds eating the baker's flesh, v.19, the dream's dark issue, the symbol of the baker's death and dishonor, hanged, the birds eating him, v.19, the interpretation to follow, vv.18-19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And in the uppermost basket there was of all manner of bakemeats for Pharaoh; and the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head
the birds eating from the baker's basket
the ominous sign (the birds presaging the eating of the baker's flesh, v.19)
the dream of death, which Joseph would interpret (vv.18-19)

schools.summary: Joseph interprets the three baskets as three days: 'and Joseph answered and said, This is the interpretation thereof: The three baskets are three days' — Joseph (by God) interpreting the baker's dream: the three baskets (v.16) are three days (the parallel with the butler's three branches, v.12, the meaning given, though, this time, ominous, v.19, the symbol read); Joseph giving the interpretation confidently (as from God, v.8) — the certain meaning (the God-taught reading, whether good, the butler, or ill, the baker, the truth declared); 'The three baskets are three days' (the three baskets, v.16, signify three days, to the baker's death, v.19, the symbol interpreted, the time to the fulfillment, the providence revealing the dark meaning through Joseph, the honest interpreter, declaring the ill as well as the good) — Gill.

themes: And Joseph answered and said, This is the interpretation thereof: The three baskets are three days
Joseph interpreting the three baskets as three days
the God-given meaning (parallel to the butler's, v.12), this time ominous
the honest interpreter declaring the ill (the baker's death, v.19)

schools.summary: Joseph foretells the baker's death: 'yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree; and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee' — Joseph foretelling the baker's death: in three days Pharaoh would 'lift up his head from off him' (a grim play on the phrase used of the butler, v.13 — there to honor, here to behead/hang) and hang him on a tree (the doom of the baker, the interpretation, the three baskets, v.18, the death foretold); the same phrase ('lift up thy head') used of both officers, but with opposite issue — the butler raised to honor (v.13), the baker's head lifted off (executed, the grim wordplay, the contrasting dooms); 'the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee' (the baker hanged, his unburied body eaten by birds, the very birds of his dream, v.17, now eating his flesh, the dishonorable death, the dark interpretation, the exact fulfillment, v.22, the honest, God-given reading proved true) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Yet within three days shall Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and shall hang thee on a tree; and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee
Joseph foretelling the baker's death
the grim play on 'lift up thy head' (the butler to honor, v.13; the baker to hanging)
the birds of his dream (v.17) eating his flesh, fulfilled exactly (v.22)

schools.summary: The third day, Pharaoh's birthday, brings the dreams to pass: 'and it came to pass the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, that he made a feast unto all his servants: and he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker among his servants' — on the third day (as Joseph foretold, vv.13, 19), Pharaoh's birthday, the king making a feast (the occasion on which the two officers' cases were judged, the providence bringing the dreams to pass at the appointed time, the royal feast); the birthday feast (the custom of kings, celebrating with a feast and acts of clemency or judgment, the occasion for the officers' sentencing, the day that fulfilled the dreams); Pharaoh 'lifted up the head' of both (the phrase Joseph had used, vv.13, 19) — to honor the butler (restored, v.21) and to behead/hang the baker (executed, v.22, the exact fulfillment of the dreams, the providence verifying Joseph's interpretation, and preparing his deliverance, 41:9-13) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass the third day, which was Pharaoh's birthday, that he made a feast unto all his servants: and he lifted up the head of the chief butler and of the chief baker
the third day, Pharaoh's birthday, bringing the dreams to pass
the dreams fulfilled exactly at the appointed time (vv.13, 19)
the providence verifying Joseph's interpretation (and preparing his deliverance, 41:9-13)

schools.summary: The chief butler restored, as Joseph foretold: 'and he restored the chief butler unto his butlership again; and he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand' — Pharaoh restoring the chief butler to his office (cupbearer), and he giving the cup into Pharaoh's hand (as in his dream, v.11, the exact fulfillment of Joseph's interpretation, v.13, the restoration foretold and accomplished, the providence verifying the word); the butler restored to his office (v.13) — the favorable issue (the 'lift up his head' of honor, v.13, the dream fulfilled, the office regained); 'he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand' (the butler serving Pharaoh again, the very act of his dream, v.11, the exact fulfillment, the interpretation proved true, the providence, and Joseph's God-given gift, verified) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And he restored the chief butler unto his butlership again; and he gave the cup into Pharaoh's hand
the chief butler restored, as Joseph foretold
the dream fulfilled exactly (vv.11, 13), the office regained
the interpretation verified, the providence confirming the word

schools.summary: The chief baker hanged, as Joseph foretold: 'but he hanged the chief baker: as Joseph had interpreted to them' — Pharaoh hanging the chief baker (as Joseph foretold, v.19, the exact fulfillment of the dark interpretation, the baker's death, the providence verifying Joseph's word, both interpretations, the butler's restoration and the baker's death, proved true); the baker's execution (v.19) — the fulfillment of the grim dream (the birds eating his flesh, v.17, 19, the dishonorable death, the dark issue); 'as Joseph had interpreted to them' (both interpretations exactly fulfilled, the butler restored, v.21, the baker hanged, v.22, the proof of Joseph's God-given gift, v.8, interpretations belong to God, the providence verifying the word, the interpreter vindicated, and the way prepared for his own deliverance, 41:9-13) — Gill.

themes: But he hanged the chief baker: as Joseph had interpreted to them
the chief baker hanged, as Joseph foretold
both interpretations exactly verified (vv.21-22)
the proof of Joseph's God-given gift (v.8), the way prepared for his deliverance (41:9-13)

schools.summary: The chief butler forgetting Joseph: 'yet did not the chief butler remember Joseph, but forgat him' — the butler, restored to favor (v.21), not remembering Joseph (who had asked to be remembered, v.14), but forgetting him (thought no more of him, the base ingratitude — forgetting his benefactor, the one who had interpreted his dream and comforted him, the all-too-common ingratitude of those raised to prosperity, forgetting those who helped them in adversity, the wrong done to Joseph, left in prison, his hope of release disappointed); the butler's forgetting a severe trial of Joseph's patience and faith — Joseph, who had looked for deliverance through the butler (v.14), left in prison two full years more (41:1, the deferred hope, the trial of waiting, yet the providence in the delay — God's time was not yet, the butler must forget, that Joseph might be remembered at the right moment, when Pharaoh dreamed, 41:9-13, and so be raised not by the butler's favor but by God's hand, to the highest place, the lesson that man's forgetfulness cannot frustrate God's purpose, which used the very delay, the trial that taught Joseph to wait on God, not man, Psalm 118:8-9, better to trust in the LORD than to put confidence in princes, the providence overruling the ingratitude for good); the butler's ingratitude a warning (against forgetting benefactors) and a comfort (that God remembers his own, even when men forget, and delivers in his own time, 41:9-14, the trial of Joseph's faith, overruled by God) — Calvin, Gill, Henry.

debates.question: What does the butler's forgetting Joseph display?

sides.position: The base ingratitude of the prospered (forgetting his benefactor) - yet a severe trial of Joseph's patience (two more years, 41:1), overruled by the providence: God's time not yet, that Joseph might be raised by God's hand (41:9-14), not man's favor; the lesson to wait on God, not princes (Psalm 118:8-9)

themes: Yet did not the chief butler remember Joseph, but forgat him
the chief butler forgetting Joseph (the crux of the forgetting)
the base ingratitude, a severe trial of Joseph's patience (two more years, 41:1)
overruled by the providence (God's time, that Joseph be raised by God's hand, not man's favor; Psalm 118:8-9)
